title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about the future of work. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Work in Progress
content.subtitle: An article examining how the nature of work is changing.
content.text:
The office, as most workers over the age of forty recognise it, was always a stranger invention than we admitted. The idea of gathering hundreds or thousands of people in a single building to perform tasks that many of them could, in principle, perform anywhere — tasks that consist largely of processing information and communicating with others — was a product of specific historical circumstances: the technologies of the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries that made centralisation efficient. [31] Remote work did not create a new possibility. It revealed that the old one had been resting on assumptions we had never examined. [/31]

The pandemic of 2020 conducted, in effect, the largest experiment in remote working the world had ever seen. The results were, by most measures, surprising. Productivity in knowledge-work sectors held steady or improved. Employee surveys reported that many workers found distributed work more focused and less socially exhausting than office life. [32] The most consistent complaints were not about work quality but about isolation, blurred boundaries between professional and personal time, and the difficulty of informal collaboration — the kind of accidental conversation in a corridor that, it turned out, had been doing more structural work than anyone had noticed. [/32]

The challenge of automation runs deeper than remote work debates. Artificial intelligence and advanced robotics are not simply replacing individual jobs; they are restructuring the category of work itself. [33] Tasks that were assumed to require human judgment — medical diagnosis, legal research, financial advising — are now being performed partly or wholly by algorithms with results that, in controlled conditions, match or exceed those of trained human professionals. [/33] The economic and social implications of this are still being absorbed.

The standard response to automation anxiety is historical: the industrial revolution displaced millions of jobs, but overall employment rose as new industries created work that had not previously existed. [34] This argument is reassuring up to a point, but it assumes that the pace of job creation will match the pace of displacement — an assumption that was reasonable for the relatively slow automation of the twentieth century and may not hold for the accelerating automation of the twenty-first. [/34]

Meanwhile, the structure of employment itself is changing in ways that complicate both the automation debate and the remote work conversation. The rise of the gig economy — platforms that connect workers with short-term tasks rather than long-term employment — has created flexibility for some and precarity for many. [35] Workers on these platforms typically lack the protections that formal employment provides: sick pay, pension contributions, legal recourse against unfair treatment. They carry the risks of self-employment without most of its rewards. [/35]

What emerges from these converging trends is not a single picture of the future of work but a divergence: between workers whose skills, qualifications, and social capital allow them to navigate change and thrive, and those for whom each transition brings new forms of insecurity. [36] The most important policy question of the next generation is not what work will look like, but who will have access to its benefits — and whether societies are willing to make the political choices necessary to distribute those benefits more equitably. [/36]

questions.question: The writer's argument about the traditional office model is that
options: A) it was always inefficient and should have been abandoned earlier.
B) it was a logical and permanent solution to the challenge of organising work.
C) it was a product of past technological conditions, not an eternal necessity.
D) it was only suited to large organisations rather than small businesses.
questions.explanation: The writer says the office was 'a product of specific historical circumstances' — technologies that made centralisation efficient — not a permanent or inevitable arrangement.

questions.question: The main complaints about remote work during the pandemic concerned
options: A) falling productivity and poor quality of work.
B) the difficulty of managing teams without being physically present.
C) loneliness, overwork, and loss of spontaneous interaction with colleagues.
D) the cost of setting up appropriate home working environments.
questions.explanation: Complaints were 'isolation, blurred boundaries between professional and personal time, and the difficulty of informal collaboration' — loneliness, overwork, and loss of spontaneous contact.

questions.question: The writer's point about artificial intelligence and professional tasks is that
options: A) algorithms are always more reliable than human professionals.
B) automation is affecting roles previously thought to require uniquely human abilities.
C) medical and legal professionals will be the first to lose their jobs to automation.
D) human professionals will need to work alongside algorithms in future.
questions.explanation: Tasks 'assumed to require human judgment' are 'now being performed partly or wholly by algorithms', showing automation affects domains once considered beyond its reach.

questions.question: The writer is sceptical of the historical argument that automation always creates new jobs because
options: A) the industries created by past automation were less desirable than those they replaced.
B) the speed of current automation may outpace the creation of new employment.
C) governments have not invested sufficiently in retraining displaced workers.
D) the industrial revolution caused more harm than the historical argument acknowledges.
questions.explanation: The writer says the historical argument 'assumes that the pace of job creation will match the pace of displacement — an assumption...that may not hold for the accelerating automation of the twenty-first century'.

questions.question: The writer's attitude towards gig economy workers is
options: A) supportive, as gig work offers valuable freedom and independence.
B) neutral, presenting both the advantages and disadvantages objectively.
C) sympathetic, highlighting the lack of protection and security they face.
D) critical, as gig workers have chosen to prioritise flexibility over security.
questions.explanation: The writer describes gig workers as lacking protections and carrying 'the risks of self-employment without most of its rewards' — a sympathetic portrayal of their disadvantaged position.

questions.question: The writer concludes that the most important question about the future of work is
options: A) which jobs will survive the automation of the next generation.
B) how technology companies should be regulated to protect workers.
C) whether the benefits of work will be shared fairly across society.
D) what skills workers will need to remain employable in future.
questions.explanation: The writer says the key question is 'who will have access to its benefits — and whether societies are willing to make the political choices necessary to distribute those benefits more equitably'.
